A1.label: Prime count via Sieve of Eratosthenes

A2.label: Prime count via trial division (independent cross-check)

claim_formal.subject: positive integers from 1 to 1000
claim_formal.operator_note: 'Fewer than 20 percent' means the count of primes in [1, 1000] divided by 1000, multiplied by 100, must be strictly less than 20. Equivalently, the prime count must be strictly less than 200.

claim_natural: Fewer than 20 percent of the positive integers from 1 to 1000 are prime.

cross_checks.description: Sieve of Eratosthenes vs trial division prime count

adversarial_checks.question: Could the sieve or trial division have a bug that undercounts primes?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Cross-checked two independent algorithms (Sieve of Eratosthenes and trial division). Both produce identical prime lists. Additionally, the result of 168 primes up to 1000 is a well-known value in number theory, denoted π(1000) = 168.
adversarial_checks.finding: Both methods agree on 168 primes. This matches the known value π(1000) = 168.

adversarial_checks.question: Is there an interpretation where '20 percent' could mean something other than 200 out of 1000?
adversarial_checks.verification_performed: Considered whether 'fewer than 20 percent' could be non-strict (≤ vs <). The phrase 'fewer than' is unambiguously strict inequality. Even under ≤, 168 ≤ 200 holds, so the claim would still be true.
adversarial_checks.finding: No alternative interpretation changes the result. 168 < 200 under any reading.
